title: Microsoft Entra ID: The Directory, Tenants, Users & Groups
summary: Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) is the directory at the heart of Azure: the authoritative list of every user, group, and app, and the authority that issues the signed tokens proving who they are.
analogy: A tenant is your organization's private phonebook and passport office rolled into one: it lists everyone who belongs to you (users and groups) and it's the only office trusted to stamp the passports (tokens) that get them through any Azure door.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  Tenant[Microsoft Entra tenant\norg identity boundary] --> Users[Users\nhumans]
  Tenant --> Groups[Groups\ncollections of users]
  Tenant --> Apps[App registrations\n+ service principals]
  Users --> Groups
  Groups --> Roles[Assigned RBAC roles\nat a scope]
  Tenant --> Tokens[Issues signed tokens]
  Tokens --> Azure[Azure + Microsoft 365 + SaaS]
body:
## First principles

If you're going to trust *identity* instead of *location*, you need one authoritative place that knows every identity and can vouch for it. A scattered list of logins per app is chaos — every app reinventing passwords, no single off-boarding, no consistent rules. You need a **directory**: a single source of truth for who exists, what they belong to, and what proof to trust.

## Why it exists

Before a central directory, every application kept its own little user table. Onboarding meant creating ten accounts; off-boarding a departing employee meant *remembering* all ten (and you never did). There was no consistent way to say "require MFA" or "this person is in Finance."

**Microsoft Entra ID** solves this by being the one directory that Azure, Microsoft 365, and thousands of SaaS apps all defer to. One identity per person. One place to disable them. One place to set policy. It is a **cloud-native, internet-scale identity service** — not a server you run, but a multi-tenant service Microsoft operates for you.

> **Name note:** Entra ID *was* called **Azure Active Directory (Azure AD)** until 2023. Same service. It is **not** the same thing as the on-prem **Active Directory Domain Services** you may know from corporate servers — that's a different, older product (covered in the hybrid-identity lesson).

## How it works

The building blocks:

- **Tenant** — a dedicated, isolated instance of Entra ID for one organization. It's an **identity boundary**: your users, groups, and apps live here and nowhere else. Each tenant has a default domain like `contoso.onmicrosoft.com` and a unique **tenant ID** (a GUID). One company usually has one primary tenant.
- **User** — a human identity that can sign in. Either **cloud-only** (created in Entra) or **synced** from on-prem Active Directory. Each has a User Principal Name (UPN) like `ada@contoso.com`.
- **Group** — a named collection of users (and other groups). The point of groups is **assign-to-the-group, not the person**: put access on "Finance-Admins" and membership changes automatically grant and revoke it. **Security groups** govern access; **Microsoft 365 groups** also bundle a mailbox and collaboration tools.
- **App identities** — apps get identities too: **app registrations** and **service principals** (next lesson), plus **managed identities** for Azure resources.

When any of these signs in, the tenant **issues a token** — a signed, time-limited statement of *who* they are and *which tenant* they belong to. Resource services validate that token's issuer, audience, and expiry before doing anything. Entra is the trusted stamp office.

## In practice

Groups are the leverage point. You almost never want to assign a role to an individual — you assign it to a group and manage membership:

```bash
# Create a security group, then add a user to it.
az ad group create \
  --display-name "Finance-Admins" \
  --mail-nickname "finance-admins"

az ad group member add \
  --group "Finance-Admins" \
  --member-id <user-object-id>

# Inspect a user and the tenant they live in.
az ad user show --id <user-upn> \
  --query '{name:displayName, upn:userPrincipalName, id:id}'

az account show --query '{tenantId:tenantId, tenantName:name}'
```

Later, you grant **Finance-Admins** an RBAC role at a scope once — and everyone in the group inherits it. Add a new hire to the group; they're productive instantly. Remove a leaver; their access vanishes everywhere.

## Gotchas

- **Tenant ≠ subscription.** A tenant holds *identities*; a **subscription** holds *resources and billing*. One tenant can trust many subscriptions, but a subscription trusts exactly one tenant for sign-in. People constantly mix these up.
- **Entra ID ≠ on-prem Active Directory.** Different protocols (Entra speaks OAuth2/OIDC/SAML; AD speaks Kerberos/LDAP), different purpose. Hybrid setups *sync* between them — they aren't the same system.
- **Editions matter.** Core directory is free, but advanced features — Conditional Access, PIM, risk-based sign-in — require paid tiers (Microsoft Entra ID P1/P2). Don't assume a feature is available; check the tier.
- **The UPN can drift from email.** A user's sign-in name (UPN) and their email address are often the same but don't have to be. Authenticate on the UPN/object ID, not the display name.
teachItBack: Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure AD) is the cloud directory that is the single source of truth for who exists in your organization and the authority that issues signed tokens proving it. A tenant is your isolated slice of that directory and an identity boundary; inside it live users, groups, and app identities. The power move is assigning access to groups, not individuals, so membership changes grant and revoke access automatically — and a tenant (identities) is a different thing from a subscription (resources and billing).

glossary.definition: Microsoft's cloud-native identity directory and token issuer; formerly Azure Active Directory.
A dedicated, isolated instance of Entra ID for one organization; the identity boundary holding its users, groups, and apps.
The globally unique GUID identifying a specific Entra tenant.
A human identity that signs in, named by a User Principal Name such as ada@contoso.com.
A collection of identities used to assign access once and have it follow membership automatically.
